data.title: S&P 500: One Day Does Not Make A Trend (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Last week had a little of everything for the S&P500 (NYSEARCA:SPY). It started with Monday's tiny 18-point range, was followed by a large drop, the second-largest for the Nasdaq since April, then concluded with Friday's rally of 1.52%. And all because markets feared Fed Chair Powell might pour cold water on expectations of a September rate cut. It wasn't likely, and as I said last weekend, "...weakness in the labour market has opened the door to rate cuts, with a September cut all but ensured."

I was looking to buy a drop to 6270 in the S&P500, which was the open/low of August 4th. This wasn't reached as the correction mostly took place in the Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ), and funnily enough, it bottomed at the same level, exactly at the August 4th low. I highlighted a stock "shopping list" on Wednesday morning before the open, and initiated 4 new positions.

Those buys got off to a good start, but I've spent the weekend wondering how genuine Friday's move was. One day does not make a trend.

S&P 500 Monthly

There is one full week until the August close, and it's on course to buck the negative stats and close higher. It would be the first-ever time August has closed green in a post-presidential election year when the President is in his second term.

Interestingly, the 6339 level is where the August close would slip into the red, and is almost where the S&P500 bottomed last week, at 6343.

A strong close to the month looks likely, and that would project further highs in September. The stats tell us September is the worst month of the year, but since they look likely to be wrong in August, perhaps we shouldn't pay them much heed. I tend to factor in seasonality, but only as a small piece of the puzzle.

Upside targets on the monthly chart are based on Fibonacci extensions of the H1 drop and measured moves. The first minor target area at 6474-6504 has already been reached and provoked a mild reaction. The next major area is 6958. A measured move similar in size to the 2020-2022 rally and the 2022-2025 rally projects 7490.

Initial support is the monthly low of 6212, which is close to the July low of 6177 and the major 6147 breakout level.

A new upside DeMARK exhaustion count is on bar 3 (of 9) in August. That means there can be no monthly exhaustion signal in 2025.

S&P 500 Weekly

Last week sure felt bullish, but the chart has a lower low and a lower high, so we shouldn't get carried away. Next week needs to build on Friday's strength and follow through with a higher high and higher close to really cement the bullish look.

6481 is the only resistance. The Fib extension at 6504 may get a reaction.

6343 and 6271 are potential support levels, followed by 6201-12 and 6147.

The recent back and forth means no weekly Demark exhaustion signal is advancing near completion. Next week will be bar 4 (of 9) in an upside count.

S&P 500 Daily

Friday's move unwound the pessimism and bearish bets from earlier in the week. Such a large rally wasn't merited on confirmation of a cut. After all, on 6th August, a September cut was 94% priced in, and the S&P500 closed at 6345. A 1.52% rally from the same level seems a bit much.

Anyway, the move was partly driven by technical factors. As already mentioned, the 6339 was the August close, and the 6343 low also came at a trendline, plus the 50% retrace of the August rally.

6481 is the only resistance.

6427-40 is initial support. The trendline will rise to the 6380s next week and will line up with the 20sma and Friday's 6384 low. If that breaks, the 50sma (currently at 6269) will be in play.

An upside DeMARK exhaustion signal will be on bar 2 (of 9) on Monday. That means no signal can be completed next week.

Drivers/Events

Chair Powell didn't say anything new on Friday, but uttered the key phrase:

the time has come for policy to adjust

That should finally end speculation over a September cut. Now that's out of the way, and tariffs are mostly settled, the market needs a new focus.

Something to worry about will surely turn up. How will the media explain a red day? Labour market weakness may become an issue over the coming months, and last week's action in technology stocks brought the "AI bubble is popping" commentary out again. Perhaps the lack of anything to worry about will actually be a slight negative. The best moves tend to happen when the majority are pessimistic - just look at Friday.

Next week's data calendar is quiet. Prelim GDP is expected to come in at a healthy 3.1% on Thursday, while Friday's PCE Index is expected to be 0.3%. Inflation data may still affect the odds of a December cut, but any reaction at this juncture is likely short-lived.

Nvidia (NVDA) earnings will be the main event on Wednesday.

Probable Moves Next Week(s)

Friday's move was strong, but we can't read too much into one day's action. There is often a rethink over the weekend, leading to a change in sentiment on Monday. With a lower low and a lower high on the weekly chart, the S&P500 still has to follow through to cement a bullish look and a new phase of trend. I think it will, and that will be confirmed with higher lows and a higher close next week. This would also give the August bar a positive look and project follow-through in early September.

If there is a dip next week, 6384 is key trendline support for the S&P500. If that breaks, the next few weeks are likely to be very choppy with several more drops (and buying opportunities), eventually targeting the 50sma currently around 6270.

I have new stock positions and have to decide if these are to be held or managed as shorter-term trades. The Nasdaq just made its largest correction since April and came roaring back, so it has a good chance of a rally. I aim to take partial profits and then leave the rest to run. Bigger picture, the overall trend is still healthy, and I expect the S&P500 to reach 7000 by the end of the year.

data.title: Crescent Capital: Buy This 11% Yield Before The Market Rerates It
data.description:
Now remains a great time to be an income investor, despite the recent market rally. That’s because some names have been beaten down so much that it may take time for them to fully recover to their prior valuations. This is perhaps most evident in the BDC and REIT sectors, with many stocks yielding well above their historical averages.

This brings me to Crescent Capital BDC (NASDAQ:CCAP), which I last covered in May, highlighting its attractive dividend yield and significant discount to NAV, providing investors with a solid margin of safety. Despite the recent uptick in price from the $14-level to $15.28, CCAP remains far below its 52-week high of $20.19, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit CCAP, including recent operating fundamentals, and discuss why it remains an attractive pick for high yield and value all in one, so let’s get started!

Why CCAP?

Crescent Capital BDC is an externally-managed business development company that provides debt financing to middle market companies. It’s produced a solid total return of 115% over the past 5 years, beating the 102% of the S&P 500 (SPY) and trailing just behind the 118% of the VanEck BDC ETF (BIZD), as shown below.

At present, it has a $1.6 billion investment fair value that’s spread across 187 portfolio companies (up from 183 last year). The vast majority of its investments (91%) are in the safer first-lien asset category, which are first in line for recoupment of principal in the event of a borrower default.

Moreover, CCAP focuses on sponsor-backed debt positions, which represent 99% of the portfolio. This helps to ensure that portfolio investments have first been vetted by a private equity firm for their business potential and viability. Having a sponsor also enables the portfolio company to tap valuable financial strategy and guidance from members of the PE firm.

CCAP also focuses on non-cyclical industries, which comprise 85% of portfolio value, and its average position size represents just 0.6% of total. As shown below, healthcare, software & service, commercial & professional services, consumer services, and insurance make up CCAP’s top 5 industries, comprising just over three-quarters of portfolio value.

CCAP recently demonstrated improving results in Q2 2025, with NII per share rising to $0.48 per share excluding one-time costs. Including one-time accelerated debt amortization cost, NII per share was $0.46, and this still marks an improvement from $0.45 during Q1 2025. Importantly, this more than covered the $0.42 base dividend rate at a 110% coverage ratio.

Investments on non-accrual remain manageable, representing just 2.4% of portfolio fair value. Portfolio credit remains solid, with 97% of investments being rated 1-3 (on a scale from 1 to 5, with 1 being the highest quality).

Net asset value per share declined slightly by 0.4% on a sequential basis to $19.55, due primarily to the special dividend of $0.05 declared during Q2. Notably, CCAP has since declared a third special dividend in August to be paid in October. As shown below, CCAP’s NAV/share has held rather steady over the past 10 years outside of the pandemic anomaly of 2020.

Looking ahead, management expects to remain focused on first-lien loans and diversified borrowers with sponsor support. I would expect for CCAP to be more in portfolio maintenance mode rather than growth mode, as it currently has a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.23x. This sits within management’s target range of 1.1x to 1.3x and sits comfortably below the 2.0x statutory limit for BDCs.

Moreover, CCAP expects only low-single-digit percentage direct exposure to tariffs. This is supported by portfolio company management mitigating the impact through pricing power and alternative sourcing of materials, as noted during the Q&A section of the recent earnings call:

We haven't seen that direct tariff impact population expand in any meaningful way, nor contract. The companies that we thought were going to be impacted directly by tariffs are certainly ones that are still navigating that environment. I'd say there's a few ways that we've seen companies address the upcoming changes in tariffs.

The first is we always look to invest in companies that have high pricing power and the ability to demonstrate the ability to pass through prices. We had a good lens into that in the 2023, 2024 time frame when we saw a pretty marked increase in wage inflation. And given that we invest primarily in services businesses, that's going to typically be the largest component of the cost structure for the vast majority of our borrowers.

Lastly, I continue to see value in CCAP at the current price of $15.28 with a Price-to-Book value of just 0.78x. While CCAP does face risks from lower interest rates, due to 97% of its debt portfolio being tied to floating rates, I believe the 22% discount is excessive. As shown below, CCAP trades at a material discount to its externally-managed peers Ares Capital (ARCC), Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), and Golub Capital (GBDC), which carry P/Book ratios in the 0.95x to 1.12x range.

Risks to CCAP include the leveraged nature of middle-market companies, which can be inherently more risky than larger publicly-traded enterprises with more access to capital markets. As such, deteriorating market conditions may significantly impact NAV per share. Moreover, lower interest rates could put pressure on NII per share, but that could be offset over time by lower borrower rates on CCAP’s own balance sheet debt. CCAP could also have conflicts of interest by the external manager (Crescent Capital Advisors), which is incentivized by assets under management rather than earnings growth.

Investor Takeaway

Crescent Capital BDC remains a compelling opportunity for income investors seeking both high yield and value. Despite a recent partial share price recovery, it still trades at a steep 22% discount to NAV with a low 0.78x price-to-book multiple. This sits well below peers, offering investors a meaningful margin of safety.

CCAP’s portfolio is anchored by 91% first-lien loans and 99% sponsor-backed positions. Recent earnings show improving NII coverage on the dividend, and special dividends add to total return potential. Lastly, CCAP’s portfolio quality, 11% regular dividend yield (based on the $0.42 base quarterly dividend rate), and significant discount to NAV make it a standout in the BDC space. As such, I continue to rate CCAP as a ‘Buy’ before the market potentially rerates it to a lower yield and higher share price.

data.title: 3 Potential Economic Landmines Not On Most Investors' Radars
data.description:
Investors continue to be concerned about inflation, monetary policy, slow economic growth, a massive and growing federal debt and the impacts of new tariffs. These are all valid concerns. However, there are numerous potential economic issues that remain off the radar for most investors and the financial press. Here are three potential economic landmines that prudent investors should have at least on their 'watch lists'.

Student Loans:

After a four-year taxpayer funded hiatus, student loan repayments and collections have recently restarted under the new administration. Now, call me 'old school', but one would have thought having four years off from paying off a significant credit obligation would have put borrowers in a better place to make those payments, when they restarted.

However, that has not been the case. In the first quarter of this year, student loan delinquencies move from 1% to 8%. Delinquencies being defined as loans that are at least 90 days past due. The percentage of student loans in arrears is just under 24%. Surprisingly, those over 40 years old have a just over 25% arrears rate. Missed student loan payments started to show up again on credit reports in the first quarter of this year as did student loan balances. This significantly reduced credit scores for millions of Americans. This will reduce or kill the ability for many consumers from financing big ticket items like vehicles, appliances and homes.

Overall federal student loan debt stood at $1.64 trillion at the end of the second quarter. This is almost identical to $1.66 trillion in auto loans outstanding, and higher than the $1.21 trillion in credit card debt - an all-time record for this statistic. Now just over one in every eight Americans (42.5 million) hold student loan debt. However, the average student loan balance in March of this year was just over $39,000. A significant and growing headwind for this consumer segment. And remember, student loans cannot be discharged in bankruptcy and on May 5th of this year, the federal government restarted the collection processes for student loan debt that is in default. This is defined as student loans that are 270 days past due or more.

Commercial Real Estate:

I recently posted an article on how the residential housing market is inching towards a housing bust, as home inventory is surging to levels not seen since the aftermath of the Great Financial Crisis, outside a brief blip up following the Covid pandemic. This is something investors should be keeping a close eye on.

However, the situation in most categories of Commercial Real Estate or CRE also continues to deteriorate. This is important as loans against CRE do not run for 30 years as they standardly do around residential real estate. Loan terms tend to run five to seven years and have to be refinanced periodically. With loan interest rates much higher than they were prior and during the pandemic, this could be problematic as trillions of dollars of CRE loans need to roll over in the coming years. An estimated $957 billion of CRE loans were due to mature in 2025 as an example.

The situation around office properties has been deteriorating for a couple of years now. This is due to falling office property values, the large increase in the virtual workforce following the pandemic, higher costs for insurance, utilities, maintenance and staffing as well as higher vacancy rates across major cities. While New York City has been successful in getting employees back in offices over the past year. Many other cities like Chicago and Baltimore continue to struggle, with Chicago's vacancy rate still above 29% and Baltimore's still above 20% as of second quarter.

However, it is hardly just CRE loans on office properties that are becoming problematic. Outside of industrial complexes, all CRE categories currently have Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities or CMBS delinquency rates north of six percent. Office CMBS delinquency rates are above 11% from less than 2% in 2022. CMBS delinquency rates against Multifamily properties have risen more than 130% over the past 12 months.

This could lead to increasing write downs at regional banks, private equity funds, REITs and other CRE lenders in the quarters ahead. Delinquency rates would likely accelerate significantly from current levels if the U.S. entered a recession as well.

Massive Re-Migration:

Then we have the complete reversal of the migration policies from the previous administration. The southern border is now seeing the lowest number of border crossings in generations. In addition, so far in 2025, 1.6 million migrants have left the U.S., most via self-deportation. That is a run rate of approximately three million for all of 2025. Given the large increases to immigration enforcement funding in the recently passed 2026 federal budget, this level of remigration will likely stay elevated in the years ahead as well.

Trying to avoid all political commentary around immigration policies, this migration reversal will have impacts to the U.S. economy. This obviously is a headwind for rental demand for the already struggling multi-family segment (see previous section). In addition, this could impact Mexico's economy at the margin given the country received over $63 billion in remittances from the U.S. in 2024 and remittances are already starting to fall in 2025, for the first time in over a decade. This should also be a headwind to services like Moneygram and Western Union.

This will also negatively impact industries that are heavily dependent on this labor source. This includes agricultural, restaurant & hospitality, home building, landscaping, overall construction, etc. All things being equal, this should push up wages in these industries and be a tailwind for inflation for wages, but a headwind for rental inflation. This should also ease the burden on many municipal, state and federal budgets it should be noted.

So, while tariffs, inflation, and monetary policy will continue to dominate the lion's share of financial headlines; there are also other economic developments investors should also be monitoring in regard to the U.S. economy.

data.title: PayPal: Stablecoin Boost Ahead
data.description:
PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) has a slow and steady growth profile with a major catalyst opportunity. The fintech continues progressing towards a dynamic commerce platform led by crypto via a leading stablecoin and an expansion to a global network of digital wallets. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock, especially closer to the yearly lows while the company repurchases tons of stock.

Big Catalysts

PayPal is ahead of the market with a functioning stablecoin in PYUSD, though the current supply is only $1.2 billion. The stablecoin is the 7th largest in the world, but it's also relatively small and easily surpassed without some execution here.

On the Q2'25 earnings call, CEO Alex Chriss discussed the quick progress PayPal has made to expand the stablecoin opportunity as follows:

With the strength of PayPal's commerce network, PYUSD is well positioned for mainstream consumer and merchant use cases. This quarter, we added the ability to earn rewards for our stablecoin on PayPal and Venmo and announced the expanded availability of PYUSD on Stellar and Arbitrum blockchains. We are collaborating across the ecosystem to expand PYUSD's prominence as a commerce-first stablecoin with a network of exchanges, custodians and other payments partners, including Coinbase, Fiserv and Mastercard. As I mentioned earlier, PYUSD will also be a major enabler of the PayPal World platform.

PayPal just started offering the 4% stablecoin reward with the passing of the Genius Act. With millions of users, PYUSD already being established as a functioning stablecoin offers quick market expansion.

The stablecoin market is currently wide open, with some projections for a market of $3 to $4 trillion by 2030, up 10x the current size. Currently, the 3rd largest stablecoin only has a $12 billion market cap, so the market is wide-open after Tether and USDC, owned by Circle (CRCL) and Coinbase (COIN).

In addition, PayPal recently announced the "Pay with Crypto" product, allowing merchants to reduce costs on cross-border transactions. The fintech offers only a 0.99% transaction rate, paying with a stablecoin or other cryptos, a substantial cost reduction compared to international credit card processing.

PayPal World provides another growth avenue with five of the largest digital wallets in the world, PayPal, Venmo, Mercado Pago, Tenpay Global, and UPI, coming together on a seamless global platform. The connected digital wallets bring the global digital wallet user base accessible by merchants from 400 million directly via PayPal and Venmo to 2 billion people now.

Test

PayPal was already cruising before the stablecoin and global digital wallet opportunities kicked into growth drivers. The fintech reported Q2'25 sales grew 5.1% YoY and beat analyst estimates by a solid $240 million, while EPS reached $1.40 for a $0.10 beat.

After the big 2025 EPS guide up following Q2 results to a target of $5.23, PayPal is relatively cheap. The stock trades at only 12x the updated 2026 EPS estimates of $5.74, while the consensus estimates have earnings growing by 10%.

Naturally, the big opportunity is a stablecoin boost to growth rates leading to multiple expansion. The fintech boosts earnings at the same time the market pays a much higher multiple of earnings.

A very possible outcome is an investor enjoying 10% sales growth with the potential for the large share buybacks to boost the earnings gains. PayPal plans to spend $6 billion on share buybacks this year, thereby reducing the share count by up to 10% with a $65 billion market cap.

During Q2, PayPal bought $1.5 billion worth of shares, bringing the total buybacks to $6.0 billion over the last 4 quarters. The net payout yield amounts to ~9% based on the current market cap of $65 billion.

PayPal ended the quarter with a cash balance of $13.7 billion and a net cash position of $2.2 billion. The company grew EPS by 18% in the quarter due to this combination of 5% revenue growth, margin expansion, and a share reduction of 7% to 977 million.

The fintech isn't even seeing the benefits yet of these major catalysts that move the company into more of a digital wallet and crypto investment platform with the ability to utilize these tools for the traditional payments business. In the last quarter, total payment volumes grew just 6%, and these catalysts are set to drive faster growth.

Under this scenario, a stock with 10% sales growth reaches 15% EPS growth on the buybacks. PayPal would earn closer to $6 per share in 2026, and a 20x P/E boosts the stock to $120.

Naturally, the payments and crypto worlds are highly competitive now. PayPal has struggled in the prior few years growing the business due to new payment options in P2P, and no guarantees exist for growth to expand to a level to warrant multiple expansion for the stock.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that PayPal has solid payments volume growth currently via a strong push in the debt/credit card business like Venmo. The big opportunity is to boost growth rates via crypto opportunities and the digital wallet, leading to a much higher stock price from both growth and multiple expansion.

data.title: Not Banking On Central Pacific Financial Corp.
data.description:
If you have followed my writing for a long time now, you almost certainly know that one of the things I love most about investing is that it gives me the ability to learn about new companies and new industries. This desire ultimately led me to dig into Central Pacific Financial Corp. (NYSE:CPF), a niche player in the banking sector with a market capitalization as of this writing of about $756.8 million. As much as I would love to be bullish about this firm, especially in light of its overall asset quality, I just can't be. The size of key aspects of the balance sheet has gotten smaller in recent years, and profitability has struggled. On top of this, the institution is more expensive than I would like it to be on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. At the end of the day, this combination of factors justifies, at best, a ‘hold’ rating.

Not banking on Central Pacific Financial Corp

The management team at Central Pacific Financial Corp. describes the institution as a bank that is based out of Hawaii. It originally traces its roots back to 1954. And over the decades, it has grown to provide a suite of full-service commercial banking services from its 27 branches and 55 ATMs located in the state. Most of its branches are located on the island of Oahu. But it does have a presence in Maui, the main island of Hawaii, and Kauai.

From these locations, the institution offers up a wide array of services and includes the provision of savings and time deposit accounts, money market accounts, demand accounts, the origination of commercial loans, construction loans, real estate loans, consumer loans, and more. It even provides customers with fiduciary and investment management services.

Based on management's own estimates, the bank is the 4th largest financial institution in Hawaii. This is a space that management believes has the potential for continued growth. This is because of not only the great weather but also because of visitor spending. For the first five months of this year, the state saw $9 billion of visitor spending, which was up 6.5% year over year. And with tens of billions of dollars being invested in things like a rail transit project, a major US Navy shipyard, and continued luxury and affordable housing developments, there is the potential for additional upside.

As great as all this sounds, the overall financial trajectory for the institution has been less than stellar. From 2022 through 2024, the value of deposits dropped from $6.74 billion to $6.64 billion. We saw a further decline to $6.55 billion in the second quarter of 2025. To be honest with you, I am not terribly surprised to see a decline in deposits during this window of time. High interest rates might seem great for businesses that generate revenue off of giving loans. But the fact of the matter is that depositors are sensitive to interest rate changes, and the surge that we saw a couple of years ago has intensified competition for deposits. Many banks that I have looked at have experienced similar weakness, though this is never a positive thing.

When it comes to loans, the firm had a total of $5.23 billion on its books in the most recent quarter. This was down from the $5.27 billion at the end of last year and the $5.49 billion it had in 2022. According to management, the greatest amount of loan exposure that the company has would be to residential mortgages, with $1.85 billion, or approximately 35%, of its total loan portfolio dedicated to these assets. Commercial mortgages are not terribly far behind at $1.54 billion. I am always concerned in this space when it comes to exposure to office assets because of vacancy rates throughout parts of the US. But the good news is that, according to management, only 13% of the company's commercial real estate portfolio involves these types of assets. That works out to about 3.8% of its total loan portfolio. Many banks that I have looked at in recent years have exposures of between 5% and 8%. So for my own observations, Central Pacific Financial Corp. seems to be rather conservative in this regard.

Even though we have seen certain parts of the balance sheet decline, one area that we have seen some growth in recently has been securities. These hit $1.35 billion in the second quarter of this year. That compares to the $1.33 billion the company ended last year with and the $1.28 billion it had the year before that. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents have been all over the map. Recently, they totaled $317 million. That compares to the $380.9 million that the company had at the end of 2024. Debt, meanwhile, is quite low. From the end of last year to the second quarter of this year, it contracted from $156.3 million to $131.5 million.

When it comes to the income statement, the picture has been rather disappointing. From 2022 to 2024, net interest income for the company dropped from $216.8 million to $201.9 million. A declining balance sheet will do that, though it is worth noting that the firm saw only a small drop in net interest margin from 3.09% to 3.01% during this time. That certainly could have been worse. For the first half of this year, we have seen a nice recovery, with net interest income of $108.3 million beating out the $95.9 million the company reported the previous year. A rise in net interest margin from 2.90% to 3.37% certainly helped here.

Non-interest income, which includes revenue outside of the core banking activities of the company, fell from $47.9 million in 2022 to $38.7 million last year. But that seems to be stabilizing. I say that because, in the first half of this year, the company generated $24.1 million of non-interest income. That's a slight uptick compared to the $23.4 million reported a year earlier. Thanks to this, management was able to generate $36 million in net profits in the first half of this year. That's up from the $28.8 million reported last year, and it represents a shift from prior years when, for the years as a whole, net profits for the company contracted.

If we assume that the company continues to have a positive year on the bottom line, we would still have a business that's trading at a price to earnings multiple that is higher than what I would like it to be. In the chart above, you can see its price to earnings multiple of 11.3. That's a bit higher than the 10 maximum that I typically prefer. In that same chart, you can see that four of the five banks that I decided to compare Central Pacific Financial Corp. to ended up being cheaper than it is. So relatively speaking, it is on the pricier side of the spectrum. Meanwhile, in the chart below, you can see something similar when it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, four of the five banks I compared it to are cheaper than it is. This number drops to two of the five when it comes to the price to tangible book basis. And another one is tied with it.

To be honest with you, I don't like seeing the weakness in the balance sheet, and I don't believe that shares are cheap enough to justify these deficiencies. Having said that, one thing I cannot deny about the business is that asset quality remains solid. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1%. That matches the 1% minimum that I typically look for, and it puts the company around the middle of the group, with two of the five having worse readings than it does. In the chart below that, you can see that three of the five companies are lower when it comes to return on equity. And with a reading of 13%, our candidate is comfortably above the 10% minimum that I look for in this space.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would argue that Central Pacific Financial Corp. is interesting, but it's not particularly compelling. Shares of the company are a bit more expensive than I would like them to be, and they are a lot more expensive when we consider the deficiencies that the business has exhibited in recent years. Asset quality is certainly encouraging, and recent results have shown promise. But on the whole, I think that assigning the company a ‘hold’ rating is the most logical thing I can do.

data.title: Robinhood: The Financial Super-App Is Flexing Its Success
data.description:
Poll a majority of investors, and chances are that most of us wouldn't have predicted such a strong turnout for the stock market this year, especially around tariff-related uncertainties and macroeconomic carnage. Investors have resumed their "risk on" attitude and piled into volatile assets, and few companies are benefiting as much as Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD).

Robinhood, the digital brokerage that is now aiming to become the modern-day financial super-app, has enjoyed a sharp surge in trading volatility, particularly in the asset classes that generate the most revenue for the company: options and crypto. Year to date, shares of Robinhood have surged nearly 3x, and though I'm not necessarily saying the stock can 3x again or even double from here, I do think there's still steam left to go in this rally.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Robinhood in March, when the stock was still trading near $85 per share. Robinhood has been one of the most successful positions in my portfolio, and while I have largely encouraged selling off large-cap momentum winners this year, Robinhood is one of the exceptions I plan to retain in my portfolio. The company's growth potential is evident, especially with the depth of the company's innovation roadmap: it has plenty of avenues to expand both in the U.S. and overseas as it takes market share from both incumbent banks and brokerages. At the same time, the company is starting to flex its profitability potential, with adjusted EBITDA surging. I'm reiterating my buy opinion here.

Before we get into Robinhood's latest Q2 successes that drove yet another spike in the stock, it's important for us to pause and reflect on Robinhood's consistently expanding roadmap. The chart below showcases the company's feature priorities for 2025:

To me, Robinhood is spreading its proverbial tentacles wide. In launching Robinhood Legend this year (with advanced charting and trading capabilities), the company is broadening itself to active traders. Internationally, the company launched its brokerage to 30 countries in the EU this year, building on top of its successful UK launch - and entering into a market with a very fragmented brokerage landscape. And on top of this, the company continues to build on its banking products for all of its customers: the company is set to release new "private banking" features, including estate and tax planning, for its Robinhood Gold customers later in Fall 2025.

As Robinhood scales into more services and more markets, the company is capable of generating significant economies of scale. The company is already on a clip rate of ~$0.5 billion in adjusted EBITDA per quarter, which is pitched to grow as Robinhood expands its slate of products.

Stay long here: Robinhood's momentum is still heating up.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Robinhood's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 revenue trends are shown below:

Robinhood's revenue surged 45% y/y to $989 million, decimating Wall Street's expectations of $913 million (+33% y/y) by a twelve-point margin. Also, the company's overall top-line growth rate largely kept pace with 50% y/y growth in Q1.

The chart below, meanwhile, zooms into Robinhood's transactional revenue: the spread it earns from trading, which represents just over half of the company's overall haul. Here, the biggest drivers of growth are crypto and options. The surge in crypto interest, especially after the establishment of a U.S. crypto reserve essentially legitimized Bitcoin, has led to a flurry in trading interest as crypto market caps have surged. Crypto revenue grew 98% y/y to $160 million for Robinhood. Investors' added risk appetite has also poured money into options, which grew 46% y/y to $265 million. And while it's still a relatively small portion of overall revenue, Robinhood has also waded into events contracts since the tail end of last year (betting on sporting outcomes and elections), which positions Robinhood not only to take market share from banks and brokerages but also from the likes of DraftKings (DKNG) and FanDuel.

Meanwhile, Robinhood also continues to power growth via its net interest income, which will become an increasingly important function of the company's growth and profitability as Robinhood shifts into a banking super-app that earns interest from deposits. Net interest income grew 25% y/y to $357 million. In particular, the growth in the company's margin balances has continued to power net interest income and represents over a tenth of Robinhood's overall revenue.

Why this is critical is because Robinhood can continue to earn attractive spreads on margin even as rates fall (which will diminish the company's earnings from corporate cash and cash sweep).

Fueling deposits and engagement in Robinhood is the company's dizzying array of products. Relatively new launches, like the Gold Card and retirement accounts, are seeing their membership bases multiply on a year-over-year basis. Per CEO Vlad Tenev's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Robinhood Strategies. We've grown that by multiples, now over 100,000 funded customers and over $0.5 billion in assets. So just a few months after launch there. We've tripled Robinhood Gold cardholders year-to-date. So over 300,000 cardholders. We continue to like what we're seeing, and we're going to keep accelerating the rollout from here.

Retirement assets are now over $20 billion, over $20 billion in assets. So this is customers entrusting us with their most long- term serious money. And that's more than doubled in the past year.

Of course, the most important metric for Robinhood's growth is that customers continue to steer deposits into their Robinhood accounts. As most investors are aware, Robinhood posts its key metrics on a monthly basis. After the close of the second quarter, we note that monthly deposits in the month of July (the first month of Q3) spiked 28% y/y to $6.4 billion, representing nearly double the deposits relative to the prior two months.

The company's margin book also grew by ~6% to $9.5 billion since the end of Q2, while crypto trading volumes also surged 217% y/y in July, which is the precursor to a very strong Q3 ahead.

On the profitability front, Robinhood's adjusted EBITDA grew 82% y/y to $549 million:

As Robinhood unleashes a dizzying array of new products, it's not spending lavishly on product development or sales; it's also driving economies of scale, with adjusted EBITDA margins improving 12 points y/y.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $109, Robinhood trades at a market cap of $97.15 billion. After we net off the $4.33 billion of cash on the company's latest balance sheet, Robinhood's resulting enterprise value is $92.82 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26, Wall Street analysts are currently expecting Robinhood to generate $4.77 billion in revenue, or 18% y/y growth. If we assume a 53% adjusted EBITDA margin on that revenue profile (consistent with the company's TTM margins, assuming no further expansion as interest rate declines will put pressure on top-line growth), adjusted EBITDA would be $2.53 billion.

This positions Robinhood's valuation at 36.7x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. I do view Robinhood's steep valuation multiples as one of the key risks that the company faces. Valuation risk is also joined by several fundamental risks. Today, the company is enjoying tailwinds from both heightened market volatility (fueling very active trading in crypto and options) as well as high interest rates. Trading volumes may subside, and interest rates are all but certain to drop, which will drive deceleration in Robinhood's growth rate.

That being said, deceleration is already baked into consensus estimates (decelerating from 45% revenue growth in Q2 to a mid-teens growth rate next year). And though Robinhood's valuation is steep, the company has a vast opportunity to offset natural deceleration simply as it expands into more markets outside of the U.S. and broadens its offering to include a full suite of banking services.

Stay long here: in my view, there are still more tailwinds than headwinds on the horizon for Robinhood.

data.title: BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

The MSCI World Index gained 11.47% in the second quarter; however, these strong gains mask the heightened volatility throughout the period due to tariff policy uncertainties. Announced U.S. trade policies triggered a global equity sell-off in April, driven by concerns of potential trade wars, rising consumer prices, and the heightened risks of a broader economic slowdown. However, markets recovered in the second half of the quarter as investors welcomed a de-escalation in trade tensions, along with strong corporate earnings reports, resilient consumer spending data, and accommodative central bank policies.

“We expect continued uncertainty, and, consequently, volatility, in the equity markets due to the dynamic nature of tariff policy.”

At the beginning of April, President Trump announced sweeping tariffs on America’s trade partners, stating a goal to lower America’s trade imbalances and achieve bilateral trade deficits of zero. Specifically, these policies would include tariffs on China at 34%, the European Union (E.U.) at 20%, and Asian trade partners, such as Vietnam, Japan, Taiwan, and South Korea (with tariffs ranging from 20-46%). For countries where the U.S. runs a trade surplus, a baseline 10% tariff was announced. Investors were shocked and rattled; stocks sold off as the magnitude of these tariffs were far more pervasive than initially anticipated. A week later, a 90-day pause was instituted, and a universal 10% baseline tariff was enacted to allow for further negotiations.

The only country excluded from the 90-day pause was China, which saw a 145% tariff applied (a level reached through a series of escalations); China responded by levying a 125% tariff on American-made goods. After trade representatives from the U.S. and China met in May, the U.S. trimmed Chinese tariffs from 145% to 30% and China cut its tariffs on American-made goods from 125% to 10%. This de-escalation between the world’s two largest economies set the stage for further negotiations and drove a rebound in global equity performance. As similar patterns emerged in trade negotiations with countries across Asia, the E.U., Canada, and elsewhere, global equity markets rallied, and market participants expected that trade deals with the U.S. were ultimately achievable, despite the Trump administration’s tough initial stance.

Trade tensions between the U.S. and the E.U. also flared up during the quarter. President Trump threatened a 50% tariff on imports from the E.U., on top of the already announced 10% baseline tariff, and then quickly paused its implementation to allow for negotiations. America’s tariff announcements would likely challenge growth prospects for an already stagnant Europe. In response, the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and other major central banks of Europe cut rates to ease credit conditions. With these cuts, the central banks of Europe signaled the shift of their collective focus from fighting inflation to supporting economic growth.

During the first quarter earnings season, corporations reported better-than-expected earnings growth and addressed key investor themes, including the consumer outlook, continued artificial intelligence (AI) investments, and strategies to navigate a dynamic tariff environment. Overall, consumer spending remained strong and undeterred by the myriad of tariff threats, which drove earnings growth. However, companies have issued more cautious outlooks and have paused investment plans. Many are opting for a “wait-and-see” approach, preferring finalized trade deals to be signed before implementing price adjustments or reorganizing supply chains.

The large technology companies within the Index reported strong earnings and continue to spend on AI infrastructure. These tech giants reported continued demand for their AI hardware and services, which was driven by efficiency gains and product improvements in the quarter. Consequently, they issued better-than-expected guidance and higher capital expenditure plans to expand AI computing capacity in the year ahead, which drove equity gains and positive sentiment.

The Index recovered from the tariff shocks, but uncertainty related to global trade policies continues to weigh on investor sentiment. Geopolitical risk re-entered the conversation as conflict between Israel and Iran erupted, but the two nations quickly agreed to a ceasefire. This caused volatility in the price of crude oil globally, but didn’t derail the stock gains that were driven by tariff de-escalation and strong earnings.

PERFORMANCE REVIEW

The Fund trailed the Index in the period, driven by a negative stock selection effect. Positive stock selection effects across the industrials and communication services sectors supported relative performance. An overweight allocation in the information technology sector contributed to a positive allocation effect which overshadowed a smaller negative selection effect to support relative performance.

Conversely, negative stock selection effects across the consumer discretionary and financials sectors detracted from relative results. Within the health care sector, the Fund’s overweight positioning led to a negative allocation effect that detracted from relative performance.

TOP CONTRIBUTORS

The top contributors to relative performance include Microsoft, NVIDIA, BAE Systems, Amazon.com, and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM).

TOP DETRACTORS

The top detractors from relative performance include UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Chevron (CVX), Apple, LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), and ICON (ICLR).

MARKET OUTLOOK

Fayez Sarofim & Co.’s investment philosophy remains steadfast during periods of heightened uncertainty. While we expect volatility to continue as markets await further clarity on the potential impacts of tariffs and the direction of monetary and fiscal policies, we have conviction in the long-term strength of the capital markets. We believe that our portfolio companies, which we believe are comprised of global industry leaders, are well-equipped to navigate near- term market challenges.

We will continue to stay vigilant and invest in accordance with our long-standing investment philosophy, as we have done during previous periods of uncertainty. We believe long-term value is driven by companies that are in dominant global market-leading businesses in structurally attractive industries operating in multiple geographies, led by skilled management teams capable of achieving resilient, sustainable earnings growth.

PERFORMANCE SUMMARY

The BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI World Index, in the second quarter of 2025.
